notes.note: Cicero's daughter Tullia had just been betrothed to P. Cornelius Dolabella, who had recently prosecuted Appius Claudius Pulcher (Cicero's predecessor in Cilicia) for treason; Cicero finds himself simultaneously honouring Appius and allied by marriage to the man attacking him.

The grove and gymnasium northwest of Athens where Plato taught; Atticus had urged Cicero to fund a monumental gateway (propylon) for it, just as Appius had planned an Eleusinion (a temple of the Eleusinian goddesses) at Eleusis.

notes.anchor: I have put Coelius in charge of the province
notes.note: On leaving, a governor entrusted his province to a deputy; Cicero chose his young quaestor Coelius Caldus over a senior legate or his own brother Quintus, since outranking a quaestor of noble birth would have been an insult to him.

notes.anchor: those should preside over the provinces who had not done so before
notes.note: A senatorial measure of 53 BC required that men who had never governed a province now take their turn, which is why Cicero, who had held no province before, was sent to Cilicia for a single year; he fears mockery if he is seen to slight the rule.

notes.anchor: chose Quintus Cassius without the lot
notes.note: A quaestor was normally allotted to a commander by lottery (sortitio); Pompey and Caesar, however, had each picked their own deputy by name (extra sortem), and Cicero contrasts their freedom with his own lot-assigned quaestor Coelius.

A triumph was the formal victory procession through Rome voted by the Senate to a victorious commander; Cicero's friends were urging him to seek one for his minor campaign on Mount Amanus, a hope that preoccupies these last Cilician letters.
